topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished oak of the waiting room felt abrasive against my skin. My hands, slick with a sudden, cold sweat, gripped the edges of the chair arm until my knuckles were white. Every minute hand on the wall clock seemed to be spinning too fast, a frantic blur reflecting the churning chaos inside my chest. I stared at the receptionist, a woman whose calm demeanor seemed to mock the tremor running through my limbs. "Are you sure this is the right place?" I managed, my voice thin and reedy, like a plucked string about to snap. A wave of nausea rolled over me, making me lean forward, pressing my palms against my eyes as if I could physically block out the sight of the beige carpet. This was absurd. I was here, seeking solace, with the person who used to dismantle my self-worth in the third grade now offering me a lukewarm cup of herbal tea. The sheer incongruity of it was making my breath catch, shallow and ragged.

[story 2]

It started with the scent of lavender and old paper, a smell that used to trigger immediate, sharp spikes of panic in my memory. Now, it just made my stomach clench tighter. I paced the perimeter of the consultation room, a caged animal vibrating with barely contained energy. My thoughts were a frantic scramble—a million discordant notes played at once. Why was he here? Why was I here? I kept running my tongue over my dry lips, tasting the metallic tang of sheer disbelief. When he finally looked up, his expression was one of practiced, gentle concern, and the forced composure he wore was a mirror reflecting my own internal disintegration. I couldn't sit still; my legs kept twitching beneath the table, an uncontrollable restlessness demanding release. I needed to move, to break something, anything, because this stillness was suffocating, a pressure building behind my ribs that demanded an explosion.

[story 3]

I watched him from across the room, a study in serene control. His easy smile, the same one that used to precede a cruel remark about my ill-fitting clothes, now seemed entirely misplaced, utterly alien. My vision tunneled, the edges of the room blurring into streaks of pale light. My heart hammered against my sternum with such violence that I worried it might actually escape. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation for this impossible reversal of roles, but no sound would form. Instead, a strange, shaky laugh bubbled up, high-pitched and completely unhinged. I slapped my thigh once, a sharp, involuntary movement, trying to ground myself, but the attempt only sent a fresh jolt of disquiet through my entire frame. I felt utterly unmoored, as if the very ground beneath me had dissolved, leaving me suspended in a dizzying, breathless void.
